title: Compare Options
description: Evaluate competing tools, libraries, or approaches against your needs.

title: Literature/Prior-Art Scan
description: Map what's already known or built before you start.
content: Before I build/decide on {{topic}}, help me map the prior art. What are the established approaches, the well-known tools or papers, and the standard pitfalls people hit? What's the consensus, and where is it genuinely contested? Tell me what I should read or try first, and what's likely a dead end. Flag where your knowledge may be out of date so I can verify.

title: Steelman and Critique
description: Pressure-test an idea by arguing both sides well.
content:
Here's an idea/claim: {{claim}}

First, steelman it: make the strongest honest case FOR it. Then critique it: the strongest case against, the hidden assumptions, and the conditions under which it fails. Finally, give your balanced judgment: is it right, and under what circumstances? Don't strawman either side.

variables.name: claim
variables.description: The idea or claim to test

title: Question Decomposition
description: Break a big, fuzzy question into answerable sub-questions.
content:
I'm trying to answer this hard question: {{question}}

Decompose it into the specific sub-questions I'd need to answer first, ordered so that earlier answers inform later ones. For each, note what kind of evidence would settle it and how hard it is to get. Identify the one sub-question that's the crux — where the overall answer most hinges.

title: Evaluate a Claim's Evidence
description: Assess how well-supported a claim actually is.

title: Learn a New Topic Fast
description: Build an efficient learning path for an unfamiliar topic.
content:
I need to get productive in {{topic}}, starting from {{starting_point}}.

Design a learning path: the core concepts in dependency order, the 20% that delivers 80% of the value, a concrete first project to cement it, and the common misconceptions to avoid. Bias toward doing over reading. Tell me how I'll know I've actually learned it versus just recognizing the terms.

title: Devise Experiments to Decide
description: Turn an open question into experiments that resolve it.
content:
I'm unsure about: {{uncertainty}}

Design the cheapest experiments to resolve it. For each: the hypothesis, the exact thing to measure, what result would confirm vs. refute, and roughly what it costs to run. Order them so the most decisive/cheapest comes first. Tell me the smallest experiment that would meaningfully reduce my uncertainty.

title: Synthesize Multiple Sources
description: Reconcile several sources into a coherent, honest picture.

title: Compare Options with a Decision Matrix
content:
Help me choose between these options: {{options}}

Build a decision matrix. First derive the criteria that actually matter for {{goal}} and weight them. Score each option honestly, noting where evidence is thin. Surface the decisive criterion that the others hinge on. Give a recommendation, the runner-up, and the condition under which the runner-up would win instead. Call out any option I'm overrating for non-substantive reasons.

title: Find the Strongest Counterargument
description: Stress-test a position by steelmanning the other side.
content:
My position is: {{claim}}

Steelman the strongest case AGAINST it, the version a smart, informed opponent would actually make, not a strawman. Identify the assumption of mine it attacks and what evidence would settle the disagreement. Then tell me honestly whether my position survives, needs qualification, or should change. Don't be agreeable for its own sake.

title: Summarize a Source for a Goal
description: Extract from a text only what serves your purpose.

title: Plan a Literature Search
description: Find what's known about a topic systematically.
content:
I need to find out what's known about: {{topic}}

Plan the search so I don't miss the important work or drown in noise. Suggest the search terms and their synonyms, the places to look, and how to follow citation trails both backward and forward. Tell me how to recognize the seminal sources and the current state of the debate. Define when I've searched enough to stop, and the bias that would make me stop too early.

title: Fact-Check a Claim
description: Assess whether a specific claim holds up.
content:
Fact-check this claim: {{claim}}

Break it into the specific, checkable assertions it actually makes. For each, what evidence would confirm or refute it, and how strong that evidence is. Separate what is well-established from what is contested or unknown. Watch for the ways a claim misleads while being technically true — cherry-picked stats, missing context, correlation as causation. Give a calibrated verdict (true / misleading / false / unverifiable) with your confidence and what would change it.

title: Build a Reading List
description: Curate an ordered path into a topic.
content:
Build me a reading list to understand: {{topic}}

Order it as a path, not a pile: start with the one or two sources that build the foundation, then progressively deeper or more specialized ones. For each, say what it gives me and why it is on the list (and roughly how hard it is). Include the seminal source and a recent overview. Cut the redundant and the merely famous. Tell me where I can stop if I just need working knowledge versus mastery.

title: Identify Knowledge Gaps
description: Find what you don't yet know about a question.

title: Triangulate Conflicting Sources
